problem_summary: 2019 MCM Problem B (discrete): design HELP Inc.'s "DroneGo" transportable disaster-response system for hurricane-hit Puerto Rico. Teams must (1) pick a drone fleet + medical-package mix and pack it into up to three ISO cargo containers, (2) choose the number/location of containers to serve 5 hospitals, and (3) produce drone delivery routes/schedules for emergency medical packages plus a camera flight plan to reconnaissance the road network. This O-award paper (team 1915782) decomposed it into three coupled sub-problems — 3D container loading, container siting, and drone routing/reconnaissance — and produced concrete lat/long sites, per-container packing manifests (~90% space utilization), and a daily 38.7-min delivery schedule covering 43.5% (base) to 60.4% (extension) of island roads.
models_used: 3D bin/knapsack packing model (3D-KLP): maximize container space-utilization Z = V_F/(L·W·H) subject to full-support stacking constraints, batch-separability (forklift) constraints, demand counts, and a fixed MED1:MED2:MED3 = 7:2:4 package ratio

Improved 3D-RSO (Residual Space Optimization) heuristic — a greedy constructive packer: corner-origin coordinate frame, sort boxes by largest-face area descending, evaluate 6 placement orientations per box, split residual space into 3 subspaces (S1 above, S2/S3 sized by area comparison), placement scored by evaluation function f(A,B) = -(l(B)-l(A)+α)(w(B)-w(A)+α) with α=0.1; improved via closed-loop feedback that re-injects each run's result into preset parameters
Container-count argument by feasibility contradiction: drone B max round-trip range (52.67 km) vs. 60.63 km hospital separation proves 1 and 2 containers infeasible, forcing n=3

Drone transport model: load-dependent flight-time factor μ = 1-(1-ε)·(carried weight/capacity) linearly degrading endurance, with range L = v·μ·t and round-trip constraint v·(μ-1)/2·t ≥ 2·Dis(C_d,D_j); objective min of max per-hospital delivery time

Reconnaissance model: coverage disk of radius L^(5)/2 around each container; single-drone angular sweep θ≈1° derived from camera geometry l_v = 2·F_h·tan(37°) at 300 m altitude, 74° lens; objective maximize total monitored road length P
Bi-objective location model combining transport time and reconnaissance length: max Z = φ1·(minT/T) + φ2·(P/maxP), weighted-sum scalarization with weights φ1,φ2
Great-circle (spherical law of cosines) distance from lat/long for all geographic distances; brute-force grid traversal of road points (0.01° precision) as the optimization solver for siting
key_techniques: Problem decomposition into a directed model pipeline (count → transport/recon → location → loading) shown as an explicit 'thinking process' flowchart, with each model feeding the next
Citing and adapting a published algorithm (3D-RSO from a CNKI packing-algorithms paper) rather than inventing an ad-hoc packer, then claiming a concrete 'improvement' (feedback loop on parameters)
Proof-style infeasibility reasoning to nail down the discrete decision n=3 (range vs. inter-hospital distance), instead of just asserting a number

Exploiting real geography as hard constraints: Cordillera Central / El Yunque mountains block drone crossings, forcing container 3 onto the Caribbean-Medical-Center–HIMA road; ports/airports constrain containers to lie near roads
Inter-container medical-package transshipment to balance stockpiles against secondary-disaster demand surges (turns a static packing answer into a resilient logistics policy)
Converting camera optics into a clean discrete sweep unit: 1 drone = 1° of arc, 360°/(needed sweeps) → exactly 20 drones, then ×2 for charging rotation and +1/4 for damage spares
Reporting runtime as evidence of tractability (5.5 s traversal siting, 0.35 s per 3D-RSO pack) — signals the methods are real and implementable
Weighted-sum sensitivity sweep over φ1/φ2 in 0.1 steps (10 cases) that surfaces a threshold/phase-transition behavior in the location decision
why_it_won: "It treated the problem as a complete, decision-ready logistics system, not three disconnected math exercises. From a judge's seat the standout is concreteness and verifiability: it delivers exact container coordinates (e.g. 18.3805,-66.5109), full per-container manifests (drones B/H + MED1/2/3 counts, ~89–91% utilization, days-of-supply), a minute-level daily schedule (38.7 min), and named real ports/airports for shipment — answers a CEO could act on, mirrored in the memo. Every discrete count is justified rather than asserted: n=3 by a range-vs-distance contradiction, 20 recon drones from camera geometry, ×2 and +1/4 buffers from charging and attrition. The models are coupled correctly (loading manifest is constrained by what the transport model says each hospital needs), it grounds constraints in real terrain and infrastructure, and it adds genuine systems thinking (inter-container transshipment for resilience). The two-scenario framing (medical-priority vs. reconnaissance-priority, 43.5% vs 60.4% road coverage) gives the client an actual decision lever, and the sensitivity sweep honestly exposes that the weights matter near a threshold. Reported sub-second/few-second runtimes prove it was actually implemented."
section_highlights: "Strong front matter: a one-page Summary Sheet with hard numbers up top, plus a separate CEO MEMORANDUM that restates the whole solution in plain, action-oriented language (which port/airport, where to place each container, what it carries, how long it lasts) — exactly the deliverable Problem B asks for. The 'Analysis of the Problem' cleanly splits the task into 3 sub-problems and a 'Thinking process chart' wires the models together so the reader never loses the thread. Notation is consolidated in a single large Symbols table before any math. Models are presented in standard max/min objective + s.t. constraint-block form (eqs. 12–13, 27), making each model auditable. Algorithm flowcharts (3D-RSO, location traversal) and a reconnaissance-geometry diagram make the methods legible. Results are tabulated (location table, two loading tables, sensitivity table) with consistent per-container rows. Daily transport/transshipment is shown as flowchart 'plan' figures. The paper closes with sensitivity analysis, a model-extension scenario, and an explicit Strengths/Weaknesses section."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Distance formula is shaky: eq. (1) mixes up which coordinate is latitude vs. longitude (uses cos(90-α) with α defined as longitude), so the spherical-law-of-cosines derivation is mis-stated even if the implementation was right
Optimization is largely brute-force grid traversal over road points and a greedy packer — no global-optimality guarantee; 'optimal' siting is really best-on-a-0.01°-grid

Weighted-sum scalarization of the two objectives only finds supported (convex) Pareto points and hides the trade-off behind hand-picked φ weights; the sensitivity table shows the answer jumps at a threshold, i.e. the chosen weights are doing a lot of silent work

Load-vs-endurance model is admittedly crude: linear weight→flight-time factor μ, and the paper explicitly ignores wind, weather, altitude effects and terrain on flight — flagged in its own Weakness section

Reconnaissance is modeled as a simple circle of radius L/2 around each container with a 1°-per-drone sweep, ignoring that out-and-back straight flights don't actually image a full disk and that roads aren't radially distributed; coverage % is therefore optimistic/approximate

Internal inconsistencies and typos undercut polish: memo says container 3 has '3 type B drones' vs. 53 in the body; a container-2 coordinate is printed as '(18.2705, 18.2705)' and elsewhere as 66.22; container counts 43/44/43 vs. 53/54/53 (the +10 spares) are easy to confuse
Drone payload packing is not optimized — by their own admission drones are loaded only to meet daily hospital need, leaving low payload utilization
English is non-native and at times garbled, which can cost communication points with judges
reusable_patterns: Decompose-and-pipeline: split a multi-part operations problem into sub-models and draw an explicit dependency flowchart showing how each model's output feeds the next — gives the paper a spine judges can follow
Pin down discrete counts with feasibility/contradiction arguments (compare a capability limit like range against a hard requirement like distance) instead of asserting numbers
Derive integer fleet sizes from physical geometry then pad them with named engineering buffers: base requirement → ×2 for duty-cycle/charging → +1/4 for attrition spares
Use real-world geography and infrastructure (mountains, ports, airports, road network from Google Maps) as hard constraints, not flavor text — it sharply raises model realism scores
Mirror the technical solution in a client-facing memo/summary that gives concrete, actionable outputs (coordinates, manifests, schedules, which port to use)
Offer two contrasting scenarios tied to a single weight parameter (here φ1=0,φ2=1 reconnaissance-max vs. balanced) so the client gets a decision lever, then justify it with a sensitivity sweep
Borrow a published, named algorithm (e.g. 3D-RSO for bin packing) and state a specific, defensible 'improvement' rather than inventing an unvetted heuristic
Report wall-clock runtime as concrete evidence the model was actually coded and is tractable

Bi-objective siting template = weighted-sum of normalized objectives max φ1·(minT/T)+φ2·(P/maxP) solved by grid traversal — a transplantable pattern for any facility-location-with-coverage problem (note its Pareto limitation)
Add a resilience/robustness mechanism on top of the static optimum (here inter-container transshipment to absorb demand surges) to show systems-level thinking
